coredata.dc:title: Assembling nitrogen and oxygen co-doped graphene quantum dots onto hierarchical carbon networks for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhen
Li, Yanfeng
Wang, Liang
Cao, Ling
Liu, Xiang
Chen, Zhiwen
Pan, Dengyu
Wu, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: We present a novel approach for hierarchical fabrication of high-performance, all-solid-state, flexible supercapacitors from environmentally friendly all-carbon materials. Three-dimensional carbon nanotube/carbon cloth network (CNT/CC) is used as a conductive, flexible and free-standing scaffold for the electro-deposition of highly N/O co-doped graphene quantum dots to form the high-activity, all-carbon electrodes. The hierarchical structure of the CNT/CC network with high electrical conductivity and high surface area provides improved conductive pathways for the efficient activation of GQDs with high pseudocapacitance and electrical double layer capacitance. The obtained N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrodes for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors exhibit an ultrahigh areal capacitance of up to 461mFcm−2 at a current density of 0.5mAcm−2, while keeping high rate and cyclic performances. This work highlights the great potential of highly active GQDs in the construction of high-performance flexible energy-storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N/O co-doped graphene quantum dots
carbon nanotubes
carbon cloth
flexible supercapacitor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21471098, 11575106, 11174194, 9123310, 21371115), the Innovation Program of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (No. 13YZ017), Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT13078), and Shanghai Post-doctoral Scientific Program (13R21413100).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Distribution of N/O co-doped GQDs (blue sphere N, red sphere O, grey sphere C, white sphere H). Most N atoms are doped as pyrrole N except graphitic N.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of 3D N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrodes.
(a) TEM image of as-prepared N/O co-doped GQDs. (b) TEM image of CNTs from the CNT/CC composite (inset: high-resolution image of CNTs). (c) TEM (d) HRTEM images of the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite.

(a) and (b) FESEM images of the pristine CC and carbon fibers. FESEM images of the CNT/CC at low (c) and high (d) magnifications. (e) and (f) FESEM images of N-O-GQD/CNT/CC at different magnifications.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, (c) FT-IR spectra, and (d) specific surface area of the CC, CNT/CC, N-O-GQD/CC and N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composites.
(a) XPS survey spectra of the GQDs, N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite, and high-resolution of C 1s (b), N 1s (c), O 1s (d) of N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite.

Electrochemical testing of the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrode as well as CNT/CC and N-O-GQD/CC electrodes in a two-electrode supercapacitor with 1M H2SO4 aqueous solution. (a) CV curves at a scan rate of 10mVs−1 and a potential window of 0.0 to 1.0V. (b) CV curves of the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrode at different scan rates. (c) Galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements at 1mAcm−2. (d) Areal capacitance of the electrodes as the current density changes from 0.2 to 20mAcm−2. (e) Cycle life at a current density of 5mAcm−2. (f) Ragone plots of the prepared supercapacitors. (g) The N-O-GQD/CNT/CC supercapacitors can light a red LED for several minutes (scalebar: 1:4). (h) Nyquist plots of supercapacitors based on three kinds of electrodes.

Electrochemical performance of all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors based on N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrodes with H2SO4/PVA solid state electrolyte. (a) Cyclic voltammograms curves at various scan rates from 5 to 200mVs−1. (b) Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves at different current densities. (c) Areal capacitance of flexible electrodes as the current density changes at different current densities. (d) Ragone plot of supercapacitors with areal energy densities and areal power densities. (e) Nyquist plot of flexible supercapacitors based on N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrodes. (f) Cycle stability of the device at a current density of 15mAcm−2. Inset shows the galvanostatic charge–discharge curves for the 25 cycles chosen to assess cycling ability.

CV curves of the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC flexible device under different bending conditions at a scan rate of 10mVs−1. Inset shows different bending states of the flexible supercapacitor (inset: optical images of sample, scale bar: 1:8).

head.ce:title: Assembling nitrogen and oxygen co-doped graphene quantum dots onto hierarchical carbon networks for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Applied Radiation Institute, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 201800, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We present a novel approach for hierarchical fabrication of high-performance, all-solid-state, flexible supercapacitors from environmentally friendly all-carbon materials. Three-dimensional carbon nanotube/carbon cloth network (CNT/CC) is used as a conductive, flexible and free-standing scaffold for the electro-deposition of highly N/O co-doped graphene quantum dots to form the high-activity, all-carbon electrodes. The hierarchical structure of the CNT/CC network with high electrical conductivity and high surface area provides improved conductive pathways for the efficient activation of GQDs with high pseudocapacitance and electrical double layer capacitance. The obtained N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrodes for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors exhibit an ultrahigh areal capacitance of up to 461mFcm−2 at a current density of 0.5mAcm−2, while keeping high rate and cyclic performances. This work highlights the great potential of highly active GQDs in the construction of high-performance flexible energy-storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have been extensively investigated as energy storage devices because of their high power density and long cycle life [1–6]. In particular, all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors have gained worldwide attention as an emerging candidate for smart and efficient energy-storage devices due to enormous advantages in adaptability, lightweight and portability [7–12]. To construct flexible all-solid-state supercapacitors with high energy density and excellent cyclic stability, it is of crucial importance to assemble different electrode materials with combined advantages. For instance, usual large-size carbonaceous materials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene sheets possess high power density and long cycle stability, but due to the limitations from the electrical double layer capacitance (EDLC) mechanism, the electrodes fabricated from these EDLC materials usually exhibit lower energy density. Conducting polymers used as pseudocapacitive electrode materials have high energy density, but they exhibit a low cycle life because swelling and shrinkage occur during doping/dedoping of counter ions [13,14]. Transition metal oxides also exhibit higher energy density than carbon materials as well as a better cycle life than conducting polymers, but they present a drawback of poor conductivity. Meanwhile, polymer binders and conductive additives are required to enhance the adhesion between electrode materials and substrates, leading to a significant decrease in energy density [15]. Therefore, many efforts have been devoted to constructing composite electrode materials, free of adhesives and conductive additives, with high energy storage density like polymers and transition metal oxides as well as good cycling performance and power density like carbon materials.

Among carbon materials, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have been widely used as EDLC electrode materials owing to their high surface area, high electronic conductivity and remarkable chemical stability [16–19]. Meanwhile, a high-conductivity mesh network of carbon cloth (CC) has been used as the current collector for supercapacitor electrodes. Zhou et al. reported CNT/CC hybrid all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors with a low EDLC capacitance of 38.75mFcm−2 (106.1Fg−1) at 50mVs−1 [18]. Wang et al. employed Co-based MOF/PANI@Carbon cloth fabric electrodes for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors with a capacitance of only 70mFcm−2 at 0.05mAcm−2 (80% capacitance retention after 2000 cycles) [20]. Guo et al. reported the use of CNT-MnOx composite films for all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors with a capacitance of 293.5mFcm−2 at 0.5mAcm−2 (90.4% capacitance retention after 1000 cycles) [21]. It can be observed that the specific capacitance of such electrode materials has been increased at the expense of the cycle performance due to intrinsic properties of transition metal oxides or conducting polymers. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as zero-dimensional graphene material have also received considerable interest due to their unique optical, electrical, and electrochemical properties [22–25]. Hu et al. reported CNT hybrid array based supercapacitors using undoped GQDs as the active material, but their areal capacitance was rather low (44mFcm−2 at 700μAcm−2) [26].

Herein, we report a more efficient approach for the hierarchical fabrication of all-solid-state flexible supercapacitors from CNTs, CC, and N/O co-doped GQDs (N-O-GQD/CNT/CC). In this design, N and O co-doped GQDs offered an ultrahigh atomic ratio of high-activity N, O, and C defects owing to the most marked edge effect and co-doping effect. Moreover, a mesh-brush carbon matrix composed of brushy CNTs grown onto carbon fibers of CC meshes was constructed to well disperse the high-activity GQDs and provide fast 3D pathways for both electron transport and electrolyte penetration/diffusion. The all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor electrode based on the all-carbon ternary system exhibited an ultrahigh areal capacitance up to 461mFcm−2 at a current density of 0.5mAcm−2, as well as a high specific energy density of 32μWhcm−2, while the capacitance retention after 2000 cycles reached 87.5%.

Materials: Carbon cloth (thickness: 0.36mm) was purchased from Hong Kong Phychemi Co, Ltd., China. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and commercially available from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. and used as received without any further purification.

Preparation of CNT/CC: The CNT network film was synthesized directly on CC by a nickel-catalyzed CVD process. In detail, nickel nitrate hexahydrate was firstly dissolved in 50ml absolute alcohol and ethylene glycol (1:1) under stirring to form a uniform mixture. Prior to the fabrication of CNTs, a piece of CC (CeTech, through-plane: 5mΩcm2, size: 1.5×1.7cm2) was immersed into the above solution for 1h to adsorb the nickel salt. The treated CC was then put at the center of a tube furnace and heated at a rate of 20°Cmin−1 to 800°C for 25min with a mixed solution (ethanol and ethylene glycol with the volume ratio of 1:5) placed at the tube entrance as the carbon source under flowing argon atmosphere. The adsorbed nickel salt will be decomposed at high temperature and will subsequently be reduced to nickel catalyst by the vapor of ethanol and ethylene glycol, initiating the CNT growth.

Fabrication of N/O co-doped GQDs: In a typical procedure for synthesis of N/O doped GQDs, pyrene (2g) was nitrated into trinitropyrene in hot HNO3 (160ml) at 80°C for 12h. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture was filtered until the solution pH close to 7. The resultant yellow 1, 3, 6-trinitropyrene was dispersed in DI water (320mL) by ultrasonication for 2h. The suspension (40mL) was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and hydrazine hydrate (4mL) was added, then heated at 200°C for 12h. The product containing water-soluble GQDs was filtered to remove insoluble carbon product.

Preparation of N-O-GQD/CNT/CC: The N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrode was prepared by a facile electro-deposition method with a two-electrode setup. CNT/CC composite were used as work electrode, Pt foil electrode as the counter electrode, respectively. An aqueous precursor solution with 0.1g GQDs and deionized water (40mL) was used as the electrolyte, and the deposition was performed at a constant potential of 2V for 3h. After the deposition, the electrode was washed with distilled water and then dried at 100°C for 1h. We used the same method to prepare the N-O-GQD/CC electrode.

Fabrication of flexible all-solid-state supercapacitors: The H2SO4/PVA gel electrolyte was prepared by first diluting 6g H2SO4 (98%) into 30mL deionized (DI) water and then adding the above H2SO4 solution into 6g PVA with 30mL DI water. The suspension solution was stirred at 85°C until the solution became clear. First of all, conductive copper foil (1.2×0.3cm2) as a wire was pasted on the surface of the electrode through the conductive adhesive. After that, the H2SO4/PVA gel electrolyte was cast on the top of the electrode from H2SO4/PVA water solution. Finally, two N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrodes coated with H2SO4/PVA all-solid-state electrolyte were face-to-face assembled together by sandwiching the filter paper (serving as a separator). The N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite acted as the electrode, the H2SO4/PVA gel as the electrolyte.

Characterizations: Scanning electron microscopy (JEOL FESEM-6700F, 15KV) images were obtained on a field emission scanning electron microanalyzer at an acceleration voltage of 5kV.Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was operated on a Hitachi H7650 transmission electron microscope with CCD imaging system on an acceleration voltage of 120kV. XRD patterns were obtained with a Rigaku 18 KWD/max-2550 using Cu Kα radiation. XPS spectra were collected using a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λexc=785nm.

Electrochemical measurements: In the two-electrode system, the filter paper (NKK, TF4840, Japan) as separator was sandwiched between two pieces of electrode materials (arbitrarily cut from the samples) with the same size. The electrochemical performances of the supercapacitors were obtained by the cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a two-electrode system using a CHI760D electrochemical workstation at room temperature. EIS was done at the open circuit voltage with current amplitude of 5.0mV in the frequency range of 0.01Hz to 1000kHz. Cycling stability tests were conducted on a Land CT2001A battery program-control test system (LAND, Wuhan, China) by galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements.

Areal capacitance of the device is calculated from the GCD curves based on the equation:
C
a r e a l
c a p a d i tan c e
=
I
d i s c h arg e ×
t
d i s c h arg e
Δ V × a r e a
Gravimetric capacitance of electrode is calculated from standard relation:
C
g r a v i m e t r i c
c a p a c i t a n c e
=
2 ×
I
d i s c h a r g e
t
d i s c h a r g e
Δ
V
× m

Energy density of the device is calculated from the equation: E=CΔV2/2. Power density is calculated from: P=E/Δt. Areal capacitance of the electrode is double the Carealcapacitance of the device. Where Idischarge is the discharge current (mA), tdischarge is the total time of discharge in one cycle (s), and ΔV is the potential window of the discharging (V), area is the sample superficial area (cm2). m is the mass of one electrode active material (g). The mass of CC, N-O-GQD/CC, CNT/CC and N-O-GQD/CNT/CC are 15.25mg, 16.05mg, 17.04mg, and 18.41mg respectively.

The N/O co-doped GQDs were synthesized from a N-active aromatic precursor, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, by a molecular fusion strategy with a uniform diameter of ca. 4–5nm in good accordance with our previous report [24], as shown in Fig. 1 a. Their structure is characterized by XRD, FT-IR, Raman and XPS spectra (Fig. S1) [24]. Scheme 1 demonstrates the atom distributions of the N/O co-doped GQDs. During the graphitization process, nitrogen and oxygen heteroatoms were doped largely at edge sites of the graphene plane at high concentrations (C, 70.66 at%; N, 16.51 at%; O, 12.83 at%). XPS analysis on the N and O doping states indicates that oxygen atoms as hydroxyl groups are doped at edge sites while nitrogen atoms are doped as graphitic N (11.21%) and pyrrole N (84.86%). Abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups transfer into benzoquinone group (with whole release of one proton and one electron) via the deprotonation and charge transfer process [27]. Pyrrole N atom bonds with two carbon atoms and contribute to the p system with two p-electrons, thus inducing electron donor properties to the carbon layers [28]. Pyrrolic N active sites may contribute to pseudo-capacitance through additional Faradaic interactions [29]. Graphitic N atom incorporates into the graphene layer and substitutes carbon atoms within the graphene plane, which enhances the conductivity of N/O co-doped GQDs. In brief, N/O co-doping can enhance the surface polarity, electric conductivity, and electron-donor tendency of the carbon-based materials, which can provide additional pseudo-capacitance, and improve the rate capability and cycling stability for supercapacitors.

To demonstrate the ability of the N/O co-doped GQDs to greatly boost the specific capacitance for carbon electrodes, we fabricated a mesh-brush carbon matrix for the deposition of the high-activity GQDs as high-performance composite electrodes. The combination of the CNT aligned pathway and CC meshes was used to boost the diffusion of ions into the deeper region of electrodes. Scheme 2 describes a two-step procedure for preparing the composite electrodes. Carbon fibers in CC with interconnected meshes and substantial mechanical strength were used as the self-supported conducting framework and flexible substrate without using polymer binders and conductive additives. Dense, brushy CNTs were first grown to form a 3D CNT/CC mesh network on the surface of the carbon fibers with high surface area by chemical vapor deposition (CVD). The direct growth of CNTs on CC offers such advantages as low contact resistance and larger carbon surface that facilitates the uniform deposition of the GQDs. Then, colloidal GQDs in aqueous solution were uniformly deposited on the CNT/CC matrix by electrodeposition (ED) under optimum conditions (2V, 3h) (Fig. S2), forming the ternary N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrode.

The morphology and microstructure of the composite electrodes were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The TEM image of the CNT/CC composite (Fig. 1b) demonstrates that multi-walled CNTs with ca. 0.34nm interplanar spacing were grown onto the surface of CC. Nickel nanoparticles used as the catalyst during the CVD process were also detected in the CNTs structure [18]. Fig. 1b also shows that the pristine CNTs feature a distinctive hollow structure with a diameter of around 20nm. In comparison, the TEM images of the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite (Fig. 1c and d) demonstrate a homogeneous GQD coating on the surface of the CNTs. The magnified HRTEM image (Inset: Fig. 1d) of the composite confirms that the GQDs were incorporated in the composite.

The surface morphology of the CC and its hybrids, CNT/CC and N-O-GQD/CNT/CC, was imaged using FE-SEM. The pristine CC consists of interwoven carbon fibers with diameters about 8μm, forming large gaps or meshes (Fig. 2 a and b). The SEM image of the CNT/CC electrode (Fig. 2c) shows that entangled CNTs were uniformly grown onto the surface of CC microfibers with a length of around 100nm (Fig. 2d), exhibiting a 3D interlinked network morphology. Fig. 2e and f indicate that the GQDs were integrated in the 3D CNT/CC network. Energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) mapping analysis of the composite shows the homogeneous distribution of C, N and O elements on the CNTs (Fig. S3).

X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, FT-IR (Fourier transform infrared) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were further used to characterize the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite. Diffraction peaks at 26° and 43° in the XRD pattern (Fig. 3 a) correspond to the (002) and (100) graphite facets, and diffraction peaks located at 44.4° and 51.8° agree well with the standard diffraction pattern of Ni [30]. Raman spectra of the CC, CNT/CC, N-O-GQD/CC and N-O-GQD/CNT/CC (Fig. 3b) show a typical D band located at ca. 1350cm−1 and a G band 1590cm−1, with the ID/IG ratio of 0.68, 1.31, 0.80 and 1.07, respectively. Because the ID/IG ratio of carbon materials is a measurement of the disordered/defective degree [31], the different ID/IG ratios are consistent with the increasing disordered/defective degree of CVD-grown defective CNTs [18], molecule-fused single-crystalline N-O-GQDs [24], and highly graphitized CC. The result suggests the presence of significant defects in the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite, beneficial for improving the electrochemical activities. Fig. 3c shows the FT-IR spectra of these samples. Compared with the CNT/CC, the N-O-GQD/CC and the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC samples show C-N stretching vibration at 1058cm−1 and N-H stretching vibrations at 1580cm−1 and 3210cm−1. Moreover, the peak at 1281cm−1 is ascribed to the vibration of C-O bonds of the GQDs. A strong and rather broad vibration of O-H bonds at 3400cm−1 was observed in all the samples. All of these characteristic bands reveal the integration of the GQDs and CNTs on the surface of carbon cloth. The effect of the surface modification on enhancing the specific surface area (SSA) of the CC was observed. By the CNT modification, SSA is increased from 95 to 510m2 g−1. With further GQD introducing, there is only a slight decrease in SSA (Fig. 3d).

Elemental chemical states of the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 4 ). The surface composition of the composite was determined by C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s. The amounts of C, N, and O in this hybrid material are 82.67 at%, 14.98 at%, and 2.35 at%, respectively. Compared with the pure GQDs, the O content is substantially reduced. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s corresponds to the following carbon bonds: 284.28eV (CC), 284.88eV (CC), 285.88eV (CN), and 287.68eV (CO) [24]. The O 1s spectrum shows two separated peaks including 530.88eV (OH) and 532.28eV (NO2). The high-resolution XPS peak of N 1s suggests that N dopants exist as pyrrolic N (399.08eV), graphitic N (401.28eV) and oxidized N (405.58eV) [32,33]. Meanwhile, the content of the pyrrole is higher than graphitic N, as the formation of the carbon-nitrogen bond should occur mostly at the edges of graphene where chemical reactivity is high [34].

The capacitive properties of the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrodes assembled in a symmetrical supercapacitor were tested in a two-electrode system containing 1M H2SO4 aqueous solution. For comparison, the capacitive properties of other electrodes made from pristine CC, CNT/CC, N-O-GQD/CC were also measured. Their cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves (Fig. 5 a) show that greatly enhanced capacitive response were observed in the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite compared with other electrodes. The capacitive enhancement is largely ascribed to the pseudocapacitive contribution, though the contribution from EDLC is notable. The pair of redox peak in the CV curves of the N-O-GOD/CNT/CC involves the faradaic redox reactions largely at pyrrole N sites. The anodic peak at 0.285V (vs. Ag/AgCl) reveals the removal of an electron-proton pair from the electrode while the cathodic peak at 0.102V shows a reverse electron/proton transfer process. Fig. 5b shows the CV curves of the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite electrode at various scan rates (10–200mVs−1). The composite electrode exhibits a very rapid current response on the voltage reversal and a low resistance of ion transport within the electrode. Fig. 5c shows the nearly triangular and symmetrical shapes of the charge–discharge curves of the three devices at a current density of 1mAcm−2, but the ternary system shows longer discharging time of 270s, while in the absence of the N/O co-doped GQDs, the CNT/CC electrode can only discharge for ∼90s. The specific capacitance plots derived from the charge/discharge curve are shown in Fig. 5d. The areal capacitance of the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrode reaches 592.8mFcm−2 while its gravimetric specific capacitance is still as high as 212Fg−1 (Fig. S4a) though there is a larger load of active GQDs for the high areal capacitance. In contrast, the CNT/CC and N-O-GQD/CC electrodes show lower gravimetric specific capacitance values (106Fg−1 for CNT/CC and 74Fg−1 for N-O-GQD/CC) as well as lower areal capacitance values (167mFcm−2 for CNT/CC and 52mFcm−2 for N-O-GQD/CC). The ternary N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite combines the high electrochemical activity of the zero-dimensional N-O-GQDs and the high electronic conductivity of the one-dimensional CNTs, leading to the much higher gravimetric capacitance than the binary GQDs/CC and CNTs/CC composites.

Cyclic stability is another important aspect of the potential application of supercapacitors. The cyclic stability of the electrodes was examined by consecutive galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements at a current density of 5mAcm−2 (Fig. 5e). The CNT/CC electrode shows excellent cyclic stability after 5000 cycles due to the stable nature of the carbonaceous materials. The N-O-GQD/CC shows a serious decrease in capacitance (72.2% of the original capacitance is retained after 5000 cycles). Interestingly, the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrode shows an enhanced cyclic stability compared with that of N-O-GQD/CC, and the capacitance can retain 89.7% of its original value after 5000 charge/discharge cycles. The improved cyclic stability is ascribed to the highly conductive CNT/CC matrix, which supports the active GQD layer acts as a scaffold, while the CNTs act as a binding agent that tightly connects the GQDs to the carbon cloth, thereby minimizing the loss of the contribution from the GQD layer during the consecutive charge/discharge process.

According to the Ragone plot (Fig. 5f), the optimized N-O-GQD/CNT/CC supercapacitors can deliver energy density of 41.7μWhcm−2 (7.36Whkg−1, Fig. S4b) at a power density of 0.1mWcm−2. At a higher power density of 10mWcm−2, the energy density can reach 29.9μWhcm−2. As listed in Table S1, the energy density of this device is much higher than that of other CNT-based supercapacitors, such as PANI/SWCNT/CC and RGO@CNT. Furthermore, a practical example of an assembled supercapacitor as an energy storage device is shown. Three supercapacitors assembled from the symmetric N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrodes were connected in series. The potential window ranges from 1.0V for one unit to 3.0V for the tandem device (Fig. S5a). Meanwhile, the tandem device shows almost unchanged charge/discharge time compared with the individual unit at the same current density (Fig. S5b). The result suggests that the performance of a single supercapacitor unit is well retained in the tandem device. After charging at 7mAcm−2 for only 18.5s to 3V, the tandem device can light a light-emitting diode (LED) indicator for several minutes (Fig. 5g), demonstrating the practical potential of the fabricated GQD-based supercapacitors for energy supply.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to measure the dynamic properties of the three electrode systems. As shown in Fig. 5h, the two electrodes containing CNTs possess exhibit a small semicircle at high frequency and a long straight line at low frequency. The straight line with small angle to the imaginary axis also reveals the good capacitive behavior of the devices. From the extended spectra in the inset, the equivalent series resistance (Rs, the high frequency intercept of the semicircle on the real axis) and charge-transfer resistance (Rc, diameter of the semicircle) of the devices based on CNT/CC, N-O-GQD/CC and N-O-GQD/CNT/CC can be determined as 0.79, 0.78, 0.71Ω and 0.13, 0.68, 0.14Ω, respectively. The N-O-GQD/CNT/CC electrode shows lower Rc and Rs compared with other two electrodes. The small resistance values indicate that the 3D hierarchical structure in the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC device established good electron transport and ion diffusion pathways for the rapid formation of the EDLC as well as the reverse Faraday reactions. The steeper slope in the lower-frequency region for the N-O-GQD/CNT/CC also confirms high ion diffusion.

An all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor was also assembled from the high-performance N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite electrodes with H2SO4/PVA gel as the solid electrolyte. Fig. 6 a shows the CV curves of the all-solid-state supercapacitor device at various scan rates (5–200mVs−1). Fig. 6b shows the nearly symmetrical shapes of the charge–discharge curves of such an all-solid-state device at various current densities. The specific capacitance of the electrode is 461mFcm−2 (164.9Fg−1, Fig. S6a) at a current density of 0.5mAcm−2 and remains at 224mFcm−2 at a current density of 20mAcm−2 (Fig. 6c). Table S2 lists the main performances of carbon-based all-solid-state, flexible supercapacitors reported in the literature. The comparison indicates that our all-solid-state devices based on the use of the high-activity GQDs are superior to reported carbon-based all-solid-state devices in terms of specific capacitance, for example, microfibrillated cellulose/CNTs based supercapacitors: 309mFcm−2 at 20mVs−1, CNT/polyaniline hydrogel film based supercapacitors: 369.2mFcm−2 at 1mAcm−2, CNT@Co3O4 based supercapacitors: 105.2mFcm−2 at 0.053mAcm−2, and CNT/polypyrrole composite films based supercapacitors: 132.4mFcm−2 at 5mVs−1. Furthermore, our flexible supercapacitor also shows an extremely high areal energy density of 32μWhcm−2 (5.73Whkg−1, Fig. S6b) at an area power density of 0.25mWcm−2 (44.7Wkg−1, Fig. S6b) in Fig. 6d, higher than that of CNT-based flexible solid-state supercapacitors recently reported. The Nyquist plot (Fig. 6e) shows that equivalent series resistance of the flexible supercapacitor is about 3.86Ω and the Warburg impedance (W) is about 3.82Ω. The cycle life test of the flexible all-solid-state supercapacitors was conducted by galvanostatic charge–discharge measurements at a current density of 15mAcm−2. As shown in Fig. 6f, the solid device keeps a high retention ratio of 87.5% after 2000 charge−discharge cycles, illustrating the good cyclic stability.

In order to evaluate the potential of the solid N-O-GQD/CNT/CC supercapacitor for flexible energy storage under real deformation conditions, the device was placed under different deformations and the performance was analyzed. Fig. 7 shows the CV curves of the flexible all-solid-state supercapacitor at bending degrees of 90° and 180°. It was observed that the high bending degree has almost no adverse effects on the capacitive behavior, further demonstrating the good stability, high flexibility, and folding ability of the flexible all-solid-state symmetric supercapacitors based on N-O-GQD/CNT/CC hybrid electrodes.

In conclusion, a high-performance flexible supercapacitor is designed and assembled based on the novel N-O-GQD/CNT/CC composite electrode. A hierarchical network structure composed of CNT brushes grown on CC meshes was used as a conductive, flexible and free-standing scaffold for anchoring N/O co-doped GQDs to boost the specific capacitance and thus energy storage density for carbon-based flexible, all-solid-state supercapacitors. The composite electrode system enables the combination of high capacitance, high rate capability, long cycling life, and high mechanical stability under high deformation. These results suggest that the ternary electrodes with a combination of respective advantages of the multi-dimensional carbon materials have the application potential for high-performance, flexible, all-solid-state devices beyond supercapacitors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21471098, 11575106, 11174194, 9123310, 21371115), the Innovation Program of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (No. 13YZ017), Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT13078), and Shanghai Post-doctoral Scientific Program (13R21413100).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2017.03.147.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
